coredata.dc:title: Biogenic preparation of undoped and heteroatoms doped carbon dots: effect of heteroatoms doping in fluorescence, catalytic ability and multicolour in-vitro bio-imaging applications - A comparative study
coredata.dc:creator: Arul, Velusamy
Chandrasekaran, Pitchai
Sivaraman, Gandhi
Sethuraman, Mathur Gopalakrishnan
coredata.dc:description: The proficient carbon nanocatalysts and incredible fluorescent bio-imaging probes have been successfully fabricated and compared their remarkable efficiencies in this work. The work describes the preparation of undoped and heteroatoms (N, B & S) doped carbon dots from the Syzygium malaccense (S. malaccense) fruit extract via a simple and low cost hydrothermal carbonization method. The obtained pure and heteroatoms doped CDs were characterized and confirmed spectrometrically through various analytical techniques. The size of the obtained CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs were found to be 4.68, 4.50, 3.71 and 3.10 nm, respectively. HR-TEM studies explored that the synthesized CDs were spherical in shape and the size of the CDs was reduced during doping of heteroatoms. The photophysical properties of the dissimilar CDs were enlightened through UV–Vis spectroscopy and spectrofluorometer. The quantum yield (QY) was found to be 0.32, 0.41, 0.35 and 0.36 for synthesized CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs, respectively. The XRD, Raman and SAED results exposed the amorphous nature of the heteroatoms doped CDs. FT-IR, EDAX and XPS studies helped to confirm the doping of heteroatoms (N, B and S) were doped over the CDs surface. The proficient catalytic ability of the various CDs was revealed and compared towards the methylene blue (MB) reduction using NaBH4. Among the synthesized CDs, the NCDs showed better catalytic ability and efficiently reduced the MB within 10 min. Furthermore, the fluorescent staining ability of the prepared CDs was examined and compared in the in-vitro multicolour HeLa cell imaging. The NCDs were found to give high intense HeLa cell imaging compared to other synthesized CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Syzygium malaccense
Heteroatoms doped carbon dots
Catalytic ability
Methylene blue
Multicolor in-vitro imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence and lifetime studies for synthesized CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs from SMFE
Effect of heteroatoms doping on CDs
Applications of synthesized pure and heteroatoms doped CDs
Catalytic ability assessment of the pure and heteroatoms doped CDs on nabh4 reduction of methylene blue (MB) dye

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis absorbance graph of SMFE and CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs.
Fluorescence excitation spectra of synthesized (a) CDs, (b) NCDs, (c) B-CDs and (d) S-CDs from SMFE.
Fluorescence emission responses for CDs at varied excitation wavelengths from (a) 300 to 345 nm and (b) 345 to 400 nm.
Fluorescence emission responses for NCDs at varied excitation wavelengths from (a) 275 to 320 nm and (b) 320 to 395 nm.
Fluorescence emission responses for B-CDs at varied excitation wavelengths from (a) 270 to 325 nm and (b) 325 to 400 nm.
Fluorescence emission responses for S-CDs at varied excitation wavelengths from (a) 270 to 360 nm, (b) 360 to 470 nm and (c) 390 to 470 nm (dual emission).
(a) & (b) HR-TEM images (c) size measurement chart and (d) SAED outline of synthesized CDs from SMFE.
(a) & (b) HR-TEM images (c) size measurement chart and (d) SAED outline of synthesized N doped CDs from SMFE.
(a) & (b) HR-TEM images (c) size measurement chart and (d) SAED outline of synthesized B doped CDs from SMFE.
(a) & (b) HR-TEM images (c) size measurement chart and (d) SAED outline of synthesized S-CDs from SMFE.
Fourier transform Infrared spectra representation of (a) SMFE, and synthesized (b) CDs, (c) NCDs, (d) B-CDs and (e) S-CDs from SMFE.
XRD patterns of synthesized CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs from SMFE.
Raman spectrum for synthesized (a) CDs, (b) NCDs, (c) B-CDs and (d) S-CDs from SMFE.
UV–Vis spectra for the NaBH4 reduction of MB (a) in the absence of CDs; and in the presence of synthesized(b) undoped CDs, (c) NCDs, (d) B-CDs and (e) S-CDs from SMFE.
Kinetics plots for the reduction of MB with (a) undoped CDs, (b) NCDs, (c) B-CDs and (d) S-CDs from SMFE.
Cytotoxicity plot for HeLa cells with synthesized (a) CDs (b) NCDs; (c) B-CDs; and (d) S-CDs from SMFE.
Confocal microscopy image of HeLa Cells incubated with CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs in bright field, excitation at 410 nm (blue), excitation at 480 nm (green), and excitation at 580 nm (Red).
Atomic percentages of elements present in the synthesized CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs from SMFE.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The above results evidently confirmed that the heteroatoms were doped on the surface of the CDs. These XPS results thus corroborates with EDS and FT-IR spectroscopic results.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Properties of synthesized CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs from SMFE.

head.ce:title: Biogenic preparation of undoped and heteroatoms doped carbon dots: effect of heteroatoms doping in fluorescence, catalytic ability and multicolour in-vitro bio-imaging applications - A comparative study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sri Eshwar College of Engineering, Coimbatore - 641202, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemistry, Sri Eshwar College of Engineering, Tamil Nadu, Coimbatore 641202 India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University), Gandhigram - 624 302, Dindigul District, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University), Gandhigram - 624 302, Dindigul District, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The proficient carbon nanocatalysts and incredible fluorescent bio-imaging probes have been successfully fabricated and compared their remarkable efficiencies in this work. The work describes the preparation of undoped and heteroatoms (N, B & S) doped carbon dots from the Syzygium malaccense (S. malaccense) fruit extract via a simple and low cost hydrothermal carbonization method. The obtained pure and heteroatoms doped CDs were characterized and confirmed spectrometrically through various analytical techniques. The size of the obtained CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs were found to be 4.68, 4.50, 3.71 and 3.10 nm, respectively. HR-TEM studies explored that the synthesized CDs were spherical in shape and the size of the CDs was reduced during doping of heteroatoms. The photophysical properties of the dissimilar CDs were enlightened through UV–Vis spectroscopy and spectrofluorometer. The quantum yield (QY) was found to be 0.32, 0.41, 0.35 and 0.36 for synthesized CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs, respectively. The XRD, Raman and SAED results exposed the amorphous nature of the heteroatoms doped CDs. FT-IR, EDAX and XPS studies helped to confirm the doping of heteroatoms (N, B and S) were doped over the CDs surface. The proficient catalytic ability of the various CDs was revealed and compared towards the methylene blue (MB) reduction using NaBH4. Among the synthesized CDs, the NCDs showed better catalytic ability and efficiently reduced the MB within 10 min. Furthermore, the fluorescent staining ability of the prepared CDs was examined and compared in the in-vitro multicolour HeLa cell imaging. The NCDs were found to give high intense HeLa cell imaging compared to other synthesized CDs.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: For the past two decades, carbon dots (CDs) have become the promising member of nano carbon family having size > 10 nm with zero dimension due to their wide utilization in the fields of catalysis, sensing, bio-imaging, drug delivery and energy harvesting etc., [1,2] Owing to their incredible properties such as less toxic, aqueous dispersibility, excellent optical properties, bio-compatibility and eco-friendly nature, the CDs are projected as a potential replacement to metal-based semiconductor quantum dots (SQCDs) [3,4]. The heteroatom doping is a more expedient and common approach to improve the optical property and electron transfer ability of CDs particularly in bottom up synthesis which helped to enhance the fluorescence and catalytic ability. The heteroatoms doping or functionalization could alter the intrinsic properties of the CDs by tuning the electronic structures resulting in the formation of n and/or p type nano carriers [5]. Among metal (Fe, Cu, Zn, Ag, Au etc.) and non-metal (N, B, S, and P) dopants, the non-metal dopants have been found to efficiently tailor the surface characteristics of CDs and extend their utility in the catalysis and bio-imaging applications [6–8].

In recent years, the fluorescent carbon based nanomaterials have been developed as excellent bio-imaging probes against the typical organic fluorophores because of their enchanting behaviors such as prolonged fluorescent glow time, incomparable photo-stability and high inhibition to photo-bleaching [9,10]. Owing to its tiny size and larger surface area, the CDs function as rapid catalyst in environmental remediation through removal of toxic pollutants and organic dyes through their speedy electron transfer (acting as both acceptor and donor) capability [11]. Nowadays, the synthesis of CDs from eco-friendly natural green sources and bio wastes has gained significant interest compared to use of chemical precursors because of its abundant carbon content, low cost, easy availability, safe, and also due to resultant value added product [12]. Against this backdrop, low cost and eco-friendly Syzygium malaccense extract was chosen for the synthesis of undoped and heteroatoms doped CDs.

Syzygium malaccense (S. malaccense) belongs to Myrtaceae family. It is commonly known as Malay apple and found in Malaysia and India [13]. It is also known as pomerac, mountain-apple or red-jambo or bell fruit. The fruit is obovoid or bell-shaped with juicy flesh of sweetish flavor. S. malaccense showed the presence of phytoconstituents like phenolic acids, saponins, carbohydrates, alkaloids, flavonols such as isoquercitrin, quercetin, rutin and kaemferol 3-O- glucoside, tannins, proteins, and anthocyanins [14]. Various parts of the plant have been used for anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, antibiotic, and also aid recovery from itching, diuretic, and edema [15]. In the present study, the Syzygium malaccense fruit extract (SMFE) was chosen as efficient carbon precursor for the synthesis of pure carbon dots (CDs), nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs), boron doped carbon dots (B-CDs) and sulphur doped carbon dots (S-CDs).

The characteristic features of obtained CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs were studied by various analytical techniques. Further, the catalytic ability and bio-imaging staining ability of the synthesized undoped and doped CDs were examined compared and reported in this work, thus bringing out the effect of doping on the size, fluorescence emission, catalytic ability and also in bio-imaging applications.

The pure and doped CDs were prepared via a simple and efficient pathway using the SMFE and appropriate precursor for doping. When the SMFE was subjected to hydrothermal carbonization, the formation of CDs was initially confirmed when the red color of the SMFE has turned into blackish brown [16–18]. The obtained final filtrates containing the fluorescent CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs were collected carefully and kept in a refrigerator at below 10 °C for further use.

Fig. 1 demonstrates the UV–Vis absorbance spectra of the SMFE and the prepared pure and doped CDs in aqueous medium. The UV spectrum of SMFE (black line) showed a peak at 269 nm which corresponds to π-π* transitions of the polyphenols present in the SMFE. The UV spectrum of CDs (red line) showed two absorbance maxima at 256 nm and 345 nm, which were shifted to 271 nm and 318 nm in the case of NCDs (blue line). The B-CDs (brown line) showed two absorbance maxima at 281 nm and 325 nm while S-CDs (green line) showed absorptions at 266 nm and 360 nm. The absorbance in these regions are due to the n-π* transitions of electrons of C = O bonds and π-π* transitions of electrons for C = C (sp2) or C = X bonds (X = N, B (or) S), respectively [19–21]. Yuan et al., found the secondary peaks in the prepared NCDs [22]. Generally, the second absorption maxima in UV spectra of the synthesized CDs could arise due to surface states of CDs which trap the energy in the excited states [23].

The UV–Vis spectra results of synthesized CDs were confirmed by the fluorescence excitation spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2 (a-d)). The synthesized CDs (Fig. 2(a)) showed two peaks at 265 and 345 nm. But, the synthesized NCDs (Fig. 2(b)) show peaks at 280 and 320 nm. In Fig. 2(c), the B-CDs showed two types of peaks at 280 and 325 nm. Finally, the S-CDs (Fig. 2(d)) show two distinct peaks at 285 and 350 nm. This behaviour suggests that the surface of CDs is containing at least two different forms of excitation energy. The intensity of photoluminescent emission is at its highest when the excitation wavelength are higher, as illustrated in Fig. 3
6. The Photoluminescent properties of CDs could be classified to the anti-Stokes photoluminescence. [22,24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and lifetime studies for synthesized CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs from SMFE
ce:section.ce:para:
The obtained emission spectra for the synthesised pure and doped CDs (excitation wavelengths varied from 270 nm to 400 nm) are displayed in Fig. 3
–6 . The fluorescent intensities of the CDs improved steadily while raising the excitation wavelengths owing to the existence of π*- π transitions in graphitic carbons.

When the excitation wavelength increased from 320 nm to 400 nm, the fluorescent intensity decreased which could be ascribed to π*-n transition because of the nitrogen, boron, thiol and/or carbonyl containing the nonbonding electrons. The maximum emission intensity (λmax(em)) of CDs (435), NCDs (401), B-CDs (415) and S-CDs (450) were observed at corresponding excitation wavelengths of 345, 320, 325 and 360 nm. This type of excitation dependant emission behaviour of the synthesized CDs arises because of the varying sizes of CDs and also the presence of a variety of functional moieties (–COOH,–OH,–NH2,-B-N,and-SH) on the CDs surface [23,25]. This excitation dependent emission property is significant for CDs, which could be attributed to the different sizes of particles, considerable distribution of emissive trap sites, and surface states that account for the red shifted emission of CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs [26].

One interesting observation was in the case of S-CDs, while varying the excitation wavelength from 390 to 470 nm, the dual emission was observed. This kind of dual emission occurs due to more surface states present in the synthesized S-CDs [27]. Sun et al., have observed similar dual emission (450 nm & 510 nm) in case of NP-CDs obtained from aniline [28]. The dual emission could be due to low lying energy level of S atom. Among the synthesized heteroatom doped CDs, NCDs showed highest fluorescent intensity compared to CDs, B-CDs and S-CDs. The lifetime studies also supported this observation (Fig. S5 (a–d)). FL lifetime for NCDs was 20 nanoseconds (ns) but the pure CDs, B doped CDs and S doped CDs had the lifetimes of 1.3, 1.4 and 1.7 ns, respectively (Fig. S6) [29,30]. By using relative fluorescence method, the fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) was found to be 0.32, 0.41, 0.35 and 0.36 for the synthesized pure CDs, N, B and S doped CDs, respectively. The above studies thus show that NCDs have the potential to be used in theranostic applications in the view of its high life time and FLQY. The QY of the various heteroatoms doped carbon dots QY were compared with the synthesized CDs (Table.S2).

HR-TEM analysis is the most widely used technique to study the surface morphology, particle size, crystallinity and elemental details of the synthesized CDs. Fig. 7
–10 display the High resolution TEM images, size measuring graphs and selected area electron diffusion (SAED) patterns of the synthesized pure CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs, respectively.

The HR-TEM images of CDs, N doped CDs, B doped CDs and S doped CDs are shown in 7 (a) & (b), 8 (a) & (b), 9 (a) & (b) and 10 (a) & (b) which demonstrates that both undoped and heteroatom doped CDs had spherical morphology and were uniformly dispersed in copper grid without any agglomeration. In addition, the lattice fringes could be seen clearly in the high resolution TEM image of CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs (Fig. S7 a–d) and this confirmed that the prepared NCDs are graphitic in nature. The calculated interlayer distance between the two parallel lattice fringes were approximately 0.36 nm for CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs. This clearly very well matched with the plane of C (002) and confirmed the graphitic nature of carbon [31].

The average diameter of the fabricated CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs were found to be 4.68, 4.50, 3.71 and 3.10 nm respectively with size distribution of 0 to 10 nm (Fig. 7(c), 8 (c), 9 (c) and 10 (c)). Doping with S has yielded CDs with lowest diameter which observed that doping has considerably decreased the size of the CDs.

The clear non-dotted rings formed in the SAED pattern revealed that the synthesized CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs were amorphous in nature (Fig. 7(d), 8 (d), 9 (d) and 10 (d)). The SAED results confirmed that the synthesized carbon dots were composed by graphitic carbon (sp2) with amorphous nature [32,33]. Ahmed et al., have observed the amorphous nature of synthesized CDs using SAED pattern studies [18].

The Fig. S8 displays the EDS spectrum for the prepared CDs (Fig. S8 (a)) and NCDs (Fig. S8 (b)) which provide the elemental composition. The CDs mainly had C (74.44%), and O (25.56%), and NCDs consisted mainly of C (76.26%), O (14.82%) and N (8.92%) which confirmed that the surfaces of the CDs and NCDs were functionalized with oxygen and nitrogen atoms [21,25].

The elemental composition of the B doped CDs as assessed by EDS spectra (Fig. S9 (a)) had C (73.44%), O (18.46%), and B (8.10%). The S-CDs (S9 (b)) consisted mainly of C (73.21%), O (19.51%) and S (7.28%) which revealed that the surfaces of heteroatom doped CDs were functionalized with oxygen, and the corresponding heteroatoms [21,25].

Fig. 11 (a–e) represents the Fourier transform infrared absorption spectra graph of the SMFE, undoped CDs, N doped CDs, B doped CDs and S doped CDs, respectively. The Fig. 11(a) illustrates that the IR absorption of SMFE which shows the major absorption bands at 3419, 2926 and 1742 cm−1, could be attributed to the stretching of phenolic –OH/-NH, –CH and –C = O moieties present in SMFE. Further, three vibration bands at 1624, 1385 and 1030 cm−1, were due to the presence of –OH, –CH and –C–O functional groups in the phytoconstituents of SMFE.
In the IR spectrums of the undoped and heteroatom doped CDs

(Fig. 11 (b–e)), the band at 3200 – 3400 cm−1 was broad because of the –OH/–NH str bands overlap (or) doping of heteroatoms to the CDs [21]. A sharp absorption band between 1650 and 1750 cm−1 were ascribed to the presence of -C = C str of the CDs containing graphitic carbon cores [34]. In addition, the absorption bands appeared between 1600–1650 cm−1, were attributed to stretching of –C = O moiety in synthesized CDs. Moreover, the –CN stretching frequency was evident in NCDs (Fig. 11(c)) by the absorption band at 1402 cm−1 [35,36]. While in the case of B-CDs (Fig. 15(d)), the B-C and B-O-C str frequency was visible at 1190 and 1105 cm−1, respectively [20]. Further, -S-H str and -C-S str frequencies in S-CDs (Fig. 11(e)) were noted at 2568 and 880 cm−1, respectively [37,38]. Besides, the functional groups present in SMFE were also detected by FT-IR of synthesized undoped and heteroatoms doped CDs with minor changes in the spectra due to doping of corresponding heteroatoms. The FT-IR results clearly indicate that the surface of the undoped and heteroatoms doped CDs were covered by phytoconstituents of SMFE with thus FT-IR spectra unravel the structural features of the biogenic CDs.

XRD spectroscopy was employed to find out crystallinity, phase and structure of the synthesized undoped, N, B and S doped CDs from SMFE [33]. Fig. 12 presents the XRD graph for the CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs. The 2θ values at 24.7˚ and 40.6˚ represent the (002) and (100) planes, which denotes the graphitic carbon cores of the synthesized undoped and doped CDs. In general, XRD peak for C (002) plane of graphitic carbon shows at 2θ = 26˚.

In the present study, the C (002) plane appeared at 2θ = 24.7˚ which indicated the moderate amorphous nature due to the functionalization of heteroatoms (N, B and S) on CDs. In the XRD peaks, the small shift in B-CDs, and S-CDs were observed, which may be due to the introduction of boron and sulphur into the graphitic structure leading to an increase in interlayer spacing. The atomic radius of nitrogen atom (65 pm) is less than the size of carbon atom (70 pm). So the doping of nitrogen in the carbon lattice does not interrupt the structure of carbon and hence there will not be enhancement in interlayer spacing. But the doping of nitrogen in the carbon lattice alters the electronic structure due to the presence of lone pair of electrons. Doping of heteroatoms like boron and sulphur with larger size and smaller electronegativity than carbon can enhance the interlayer spacing [39]. Moreover, the results of the x-ray diffraction study evidently confirmed that the prepared CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs had moderate graphitic structure [21,40–42].

The Raman spectra typically had the defective (D) bands and graphitic (G) bands indicating the sp3 defects in the graphitic carbon and vibrations occurred in the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms [21]. With the introduction of doped atoms in the carbon dots system, a small redshift of the G band position is observed. The redshift indicates a tensile strain in the lattice, which can be caused by the doped atoms. In addition, the doping of electron-rich N, B, and S atoms increases the electronic charge, may result in the redshift of G band [43].

The ID and IG are the defective and graphitic band intensities and the ratio between these intensities (ID/ IG ) could be less than 1 for ideal graphite. The ID and IG values were found to be 0.59, 0.63, 0.60, 0.51 for undoped, N, B and S doped CDs, respectively and the intensity of ID was less than the intensity of IG (ID < IG ) (From (Fig. 13 )).

The higher ID/IG value in NCDs reveals the higher density of Raman active edge sites and reduction in the crystallinity of the NCDs as compared to undoped CDs. The higher density of edge sites NCDs is consistent with its smaller size than the undoped GQDs. The Raman results thus demonstrated the moderate graphitic structure of the synthesized CDs and this clearly revealed that the minimum quantity of carbonaceous impurities (sp3 defects) could be present during the production of CDs, N, B and S doped CDs from SMFE [44–46].

The elemental details, elemental mass and their electronic states of the phyto-synthesized CDs were investigated by X-ray photo electron spectroscopic technique. Figs. S8 - S11 display the complete XPS spectra for the prepared CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs from SMFE and their deconvulated XPS spectra for the elements of carbon (C1s), nitrogen (N1s), oxygen (O1s), boron (B1s) and sulphur (S2p), respectively. In the survey spectrum (full scan) of the prepared CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs (Figs. S10 (a), S11 (a), S12 (a) and S13 (a)) two distinct peaks were seen at 282.4, and 530.5 eV, for elemental carbon and oxygen, respectively.

In addition, one more peak arises in full survey spectrum of un-doped and heteroatom doped CDs at 397.6, 191.2 and 162.4 eV, ascribed to the existence of elemental nitrogen, boron and sulphur, respectively. In the case of CDs, the deconvulated C1s (Fig. S8 (b)) showed four different peaks which appeared at 282.5 eV for CH, 284.7 eV for C = C (sp2), 290.6 eV for -COO groups and 293.3 eV which was attributed to π electron delocalization. While in the case of NCDs (Fig. S11 (b)) three distinct peaks appeared on 284.9 eV, 286 eV and 288.1 eV, corresponding to -C = C (sp2), -C = N and -C = O functional groups, respectively [21,36,44]. But, in the case of B-CDs (Fig. S12 (b)) the deconvulated C1s showed three dissimilar peaks at 282.6 eV for -C-H, 284.1 eV for C = C (sp2)) and 285.3 eV for -C-N groups and four distinctive peaks appeared at 284.8, 286, 288 and 290.6 eV which corresponds to C = C (sp2), -C-N, -C = O, and π- π* electron delocalization for S-CDs (Fig. S13 (b)) [20,21,36,45]. Both O1s deconvolution spectrum of CDs (Fig. S10 (c)) and NCDs (Fig. S11 (d)) showed two binding energy peaks at 528.8, 530 eV and 531.3, 532.4 eV for -C-OC, -C-O, -C = O, COH/-C-OC groups, respectively [47]. But, in the case of B-CDs (Fig. S12 (c)) and S-CDs (Fig. S13 (c)), the deconvoluted O1s spectrum had three distinct peaks at 529.85, 530.8, 531.2 eV which were correlated to -C-O, -C = O, and OHC = O groups positioned on surface of the B-CDs and S-CDs, respectively [46–48]. Thus, the heteroatoms doped on the CDs were confirmed by the deconvolution spectra of N1s, B1s and S2p of the synthesized N, B and S doped CDs from SMFE, respectively. In XPS spectrum of NCDs, the nitrogen doped on CDs were confirmed by (Fig. S11 (c)) the presence of peaks in deconvolution spectrum of N1s at 396.3, and 399.5 eV, corresponds to pyrrolic nitrogen and CN-C groups located in NCDs surface, respectively [47,48].

Fig. S12 (d) demonstrates the deconvoluted B1s spectrum which shows three peaks corresponding to B-C, B-CO2 and B-O groups at190.7, 192.3 and 192.8 eV respectively [20,45]. The deconvolution spectrum of S2p (Fig. S13 (d)) showed five binding energy peaks at 161.6, 162.7, 166.1,166.4 and 167.6 eV which were attributed to S-H, S-C (S2p3/2), C-SO, -SO3, and S2p1/2 groups, respectively [35,49,50]. Especially, one of the C1s peak appeared at 284–285 eV (in undoped and heteroatoms doped CDs) which revealed that the synthesized CDs mainly consist of sp2 carbon atoms [36]. The XPS results thus clearly suggested the presence of the dopant element and also the amount of elements (C, O, N, B and S) present in the various CDs (Table 1 .)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of heteroatoms doping on CDs

ce:list-item.ce:para: The heteroatoms doped on the CDs influenced the size, fluorescence intensity, FLQY, catalytic ability and bio-imaging staining applications. The comparison of sizes of various CDs revealed that the size of the CDs decreased by the doping of heteroatoms such as N, B and S. This leads to change in surface to volume ratio and enhances the reactivity of the CDs. The fluorescence intensity of hetero atom doped CDs were higher than undoped CDs owing to non bonding electrons present in the heteroatoms. The fluorescence lifetime time studies supported the above observations. The quantum yield also significantly increased on account of doping of heteroatoms compared to undoped CDs. Finally, the heteroatoms doping could increase the zeta potential values due to the presence of negatively charged carboxylic and hydroxyl groups were occupied in the CDs surface which increased the zeta potential and stability of the CDs. The results of the above studies clearly indicated that the heteroatoms doped on the CDs could influence the size, fluorescence intensity, quantum yield and zeta potential of the CDs.
The major characteristics of synthesized CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs are shown in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of synthesized pure and heteroatoms doped CDs
Catalytic ability assessment of the pure and heteroatoms doped CDs on nabh4 reduction of methylene blue (MB) dye
ce:section.ce:para: The evaluation of catalytic ability of the pure and heteroatoms doped CDs on the NaBH4 reduction of MB is done adopting the procedure as described in SI 1.5. Fig. 14 (a) provides the UV–Vis representation of MB reduced by NaBH4 without biogenic catalyst for 2 h. The absorption maxima (λ max) value of pure MB was 664 nm [51]. The intensity of MB slightly decreased with the addition of NaBH4, but there was no significant change observed with the increase of reaction time. Fig. 14(b), 14 (c), 14 (d), and 14 (e) demonstrate decline the intensity of absorption upon reduction by NaBH4 in the presence of biogenic catalysts (CDs, NCDs, B-CDs, and S-CDs) synthesized from SMFE.

The reduction of MB was carried out separately for each of the synthesized CDs for the comparison of their catalytic activity. After the addition of 10 μL each of biogenic catalyst individually to the newly prepared MB and NaBH4 mixture, the intensity of the absorption gradually diminished at different intervals of time.

In Fig. 14(b), the intensity of the MB decreased slowly, later than the adding up of 10 μL undoped biogenic catalyst (CDs) to the newly prepared MB+NaBH4 mixture and the reaction was completed at 45 min. The reduction reaction was completed rapidly within 10 min due to the mixing of NCDs (10 μL) to the instantly prepared MB with NaBH4 mixture (Fig. 14(c)).

In the case of B-CDs the reduction was completed in 25 min while S-CDs could only bring about complete reduction in 35 min (Fig. 14(d) & (Fig. 14(e)). The corresponding kinetics of the CDs, NCDs, B-CDs, and S-CDs was shown Fig. 15 , which clearly reveals that NCDs is the better catalyst than CDs, B-CDs, and S-CDs.
The results that demonstrated the catalytic ability of the doped CDs and comparison of results brought out the supremacy of NCDs. This work was compared with reported works and shown in Table S3.

The harmful MB dye reduction was done by biogenic catalysts (CDs, NCDs, B-CDs, and S-CDs) and the reaction pathway can be detailed by Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanism. The BH4¯ ions from NaBH4 and MB were initially adsorbed on catalyst (CDs) surface and the electrons from BH4¯ ions transferred through CDs. Here, the CDs act as mediator for transferring electron from BH4¯ ions to MB, and this could lower the activation energy among the reactants and the products [52–54]. In addition, the huge amount of H2 gas was released as byproduct. In particular, NCDs exhibited high catalytic ability due to the defects at the surface and edges of NCDs which acting as catalytic sites and thus encouraged the electron transfer process to speed up the reduction reaction [55]. The incorporation of nitrogen atom with extra electrons into carbon structures, leads to the liberal donation of electrons to the delocalized π system of carbon which inturn could improve the interaction between carbon frameworks to new foreign molecules and enhance the rate of the reaction [56–58]. In the case of B-CDs, the electronic nature of boron resembles the carbon atom with fewer defects. The sulphur atom is larger than carbon in size and thus the electronegativity among them is similar (S-2.58 & C-2.55), thus the electron transfer mechanisms are easy in S than C in the S-CDs. But in the case of undoped CDs the above phenomena are absent and only the bare carbon sites play a role in the reduction of MB dye. Due to the above reasons N doped CDs could ensure the efficient transfer of electrons. Zeta potential measurement of NCDs showed highest negative zeta potential (−29.5 mV) to support above statement. This exceptional catalytic behaviour of NCDs thus could be ascribed to the electron transfer ability of the synthesized NCDs.

One of the most fascinating and unique fluorescent properties of the CDs is that they could be engaged profitably by using them as suitable staining probes for biomedical applications. In this study, MTT assay has been used for the evaluation of bio-imaging potential and cytotoxic studies with HeLa cells [21,25]. The HeLa cells were separately developed by the addition of CDs, NCDs, B-CDs, and S-CDs (50 µg/mL) for the incubatory period of 24 h. After 24 h incubtion, the cells were throughly washed using PBS (phosphate buffer solution) and their flurescent properties were investigated by Confocal Laser Scanning Fluorescence Microscopy (CLSFM).

The results of the cytotoxicity studies showed that there was 100% cell viability of HeLa cells when CDs were added minimally (10 µg/mL). Upon increase of concentration, the cell viability slowly decreased. At high concentration (50 µg/mL), > 70% of cell viability was observed in HeLa cells (Fig. 16 (a–d). The cytotoxicity results thus evidently indicated the less cytotoxicity and good biocompatible nature of the synthesized undoped and heteroatoms doped CDs [19,21]. Furthermore, the high water solubility, excellent fluorescent ability and less cytotoxicity enable undoped and heteroatoms doped CDs to be the excellent fluorescent staining probes for cell imaging [21,46].

The Fig. 17 shows the CLSFM images of the incubated HeLa cells with synthesized undoped and heteroatoms doped CDs. The Fig. 17(A), (E), (I), and (M) showed the bright field image of HeLa cells with CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs, respectively. The Fig. 17(B), (F), (J), and (N) are the fluorescent microscopic images of HeLa cells when excited at 410 nm (blue) with synthesized CDs. The, Fig. 17(C), (G), (K), and (O) illustrate the fluorescent microscopic images of HeLa cells when excitation was at 480 nm (green) with synthesized CDs. Finally, the Fig. 17(D), (H), (L), and (P) demonstrates the fluorescent microscopic images of HeLa cells when there was excitation at 580 nm (red) with synthesized CDs. These images clearly described that the cellular uptakes of the synthesized undoped and heteroatoms doped CDs. Moreover, the CLSFM was done capturing blue, green and red fluorescent images of HeLa cells at their corresponding excitation wavelengths. The results revealed that the synthesized undoped and heteroatoms doped CDs, could easily penetrate into the HeLa cell walls and get diffused homogeneously. Among all, the NCDs possess brightened HeLa cell image in all three excitation regions due to its high fluorescent nature. From these observations, it is evidently proved that the synthesized CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs from SMFE could be beneficially utilized as proficient alternate staining agent for bio-imaging applications with NCDs being the ones with profound fluorescent nature.

Thus in the present study, Facile hydrothermal carbonization method was used to synthesize CDs, NCDs, B-CDs and S-CDs from SMFE. The structural morphological features and size of the undoped and heteroatoms doped CDs were determined using HR-TEM. The mean size of the synthesized pure CDs, N, B and S doped CDs were found to be 4.68, 4.50, 3.71 and 3.10 nm respectively and spherical in shape. XRD and Raman spectral studies validated the graphitic identity of the undoped and heteroatoms doped CDs. The doping of heteroatoms and the surface functional moieties present on the synthesized diverse CDs were confirmed through various spectroscopic analysis. The enhanced catalytic behaviour of the synthesized undoped and heteroatoms doped CDs from SMFE were successfully revealed through the MB reduction by NaBH4. The NCDs possess superior catalytic ability which concludes the MB reduction by 10 min. The determined rate constant for the reduction of MB is 0.2666 min−1 which showed higher catalytic activity of the NCDs. The fluorescent characteristics of the pure CDs, N, B and S doped CDs can be successfully utilized for fluorescent staining purposes in the bio-imaging of the HeLa cells. Here also among the heteroatoms doped CDs, NCDs play an efficient role due to its high fluorescent nature to produce highly contrast HeLa cell images. Together, these preliminary observations signify that the developed NCDs are biocompatible systems that can deliver environmental remediation and bioimaging applications.

Velusamy Arul: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Pitchai Chandrasekaran: Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Visualization. Gandhi Sivaraman: Validation, Visualization. Mathur Gopalakrishnan Sethuraman: Validation, Supervision.
